examples.content:
<ps>
Subject: Government and Public Administration – Bad example  I have always been interested in understanding how governments work and how public policies can improve people’s lives. My family’s values of responsibility and justice inspired me to explore these topics deeply. This is why I am excited to join the Government and Public Administration program at the Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK). Since I was young, I have been curious about how political systems solve problems and help communities. Conversations with my grandfather, a local politician, and my brother, who worked at the European Parliament, helped me see how policies are created and why they matter. These experiences made me want to learn more about how governments can promote fairness and progress. In high school, I chose the International Baccalaureate (IB) program, where I studied historical political systems and policies in detail. This gave me the skills to understand and analyze how governments work and how public policies are shaped. It also taught me to think critically about the challenges leaders face. I am eager to build on this knowledge at CUHK and learn more about governance and public administration. Outside the classroom, I have taken on leadership roles to make a difference in my community. I started the Study Abroad Association at my school to help students find universities that fit their goals, while also encouraging global awareness. I also took part in humanitarian projects in Africa and Ukraine, which showed me how public administration plays a key role in solving global problems. These experiences motivated me to contribute to making policies that create positive change. Sports have also taught me important skills for both academics and public administration. As a coach and international football athlete, I learned about teamwork, leadership, and communication. These skills help me work with others to solve problems and achieve shared goals. I am excited about CUHK’s Government and Public Administration program because it focuses on both local and global issues. I believe this program will help me develop the knowledge and skills to make meaningful contributions to society. I look forward to learning from CUHK’s professors and students while sharing my own experiences and ideas. I am confident this program will prepare me to make a real difference in the world. Total: 19/35     
</ps>

**Scores**:
<scores>
{"factor1": 5, "factor2": 4, "factor3": 3, "factor4": 3, "factor5": 4}
</scores>

{"factor1": "While the statement mentions a general interest in governance and public policies, it lacks depth and specificity. The references to family members in politics and public administration are superficial and fail to explore how these influenced the candidate's aspirations. CUHK is mentioned, but the connection to its unique offerings is weak.", "factor2": "The candidate mentions starting the Study Abroad Association and participating in humanitarian projects but does not explain their specific roles or the impact of these experiences. These examples feel underdeveloped and miss the opportunity to connect them to public administration.", "factor3": "Leadership, teamwork, and communication are referenced through coaching and humanitarian work. However, these are only described briefly, without concrete examples of challenges faced or how they apply to public administration.", "factor4": "The statement is logically structured but overly short, leaving sections underdeveloped. Transitions between paragraphs are abrupt, and points are introduced without sufficient explanation. For example, humanitarian work is mentioned but not tied back to governance or policy.", "factor5": "The language is professional and error-free, but the tone is overly general, and certain phrases (e.g., \u201cmake a real difference in the world\u201d) are clich\u00e9d. A more personalized and specific narrative would make the statement more engaging."}

<ps>
Subject: History – Strong  The past is never fixed. History is always open to discussion and revision, and our perception of the past is inextricably linked to the ideas of the people interpreting it. History is one of the central passions in my life and I am determined to study it to as high a level as possible, in order to gain enough understanding to form my own opinions of past events. I am fascinated by the more personal side of the subject, and love studying primary sources to gain an understanding of how it was to live in another time. Studying History at school has given me a greater awareness of the links between events, as well as their relevance to the present. Topics such as Italian nationalism and fascism, the road to World War Two and British social welfare have given me a more rounded, if still incomplete view of some of the key issues in early 20th Century Europe. The growth of Italian nationalism from unification to the rise of fascism, for example, clearly shows how an arguably positive movement can change, given the right conditions, and remains a highly relevant example - and warning - for the modern world. Recently, one aspect of History that has excited me is the evolution of beliefs and cultures. This has been partially encouraged by reading "The Cruise of HMS Challenger" by WJJ Spry. The account has given me a fascinating insight into Victorian attitudes towards foreign cultures, as well as giving a different perspective on society in Meiji Japan, a topic I am studying this year, from the point of view of a Western visitor to the country. As well as giving me a love of studying past people and events, History has also strengthened my interest in the study of present day powers, causes and effects: Politics. My first real exposure to politics was the 2003 anti-Iraq War protests, and since then I have been interested in the effect that ordinary people can have on government and world affairs. "Naming the Dead" by activist Maya Ann Evans furthered my interest in this, as well as the more domestic consequences of the "war on terror" and the fine line that exists between security and intrusion, a topic on which my Modern Studies dissertation is based this year. I also find the ins and outs of government fascinating, and enjoy seeing the influence of individual politicians on the wider parliamentary context. An enjoyment of the rigour of academic work has contributed to my desire to study these subjects. I love to learn and to discuss ideas, and have enjoyed writing since starting school. In S5 and S6 especially, I have found a genuine satisfaction in independent study and essay writing. Several work experience placements have also encouraged my interests. Last year I worked for a week with a curator at the National Museum of Scotland. I particularly enjoyed working with the artefacts, as tangible 'pieces of history,' and also came across the Challenger expedition for the first time. In 2008 I shadowed an MSP in the Scottish Parliament for a week, and enjoyed the experience so much that I went back last June for a short placement at the Parliament's information centre. Outside my studies, I am involved in a wide range of activities. One of my main passions is music, and I play flute and violin. My interest in French led me to initiate an exchange last summer and this has made me want to travel more widely in the future. I also like to be politically aware and was co-convenor of the [exact name edited out, but a local youth anti-war group] branch in 2008, which taught me a lot about organisation. These commitments have given me the ability to manage my time, to work and think independently and to self-motivate. I believe that I will flourish in a university setting, and will work very hard to achieve the best I possibly can.  Total: 31 /35       
</ps>

**Scores**:
<scores>
{"factor1": 9, "factor2": 8, "factor3": 4, "factor4": 5, "factor5": 5}
</scores>

{"factor1": "The candidate\u2019s passion for history and politics is clearly articulated through detailed examples, such as Italian nationalism and their participation in anti-Iraq War protests. The mention of books like The Cruise of HMS Challenger and Naming the Dead shows independent engagement. Although the connection to future academic aspirations could be more explicitly stated.", "factor2": "Work experience placements (e.g., National Museum of Scotland, Scottish Parliament) are relevant and show initiative, but improved if they explored the candidate\u2019s specific contributions and what they learned from these experiences. References to co-convening an anti-war group are interesting.", "factor3": "The statement highlights independence, time management, and organizational skills through schoolwork, extracurriculars, and work experience. However, the connection between these qualities and their success in studying history and politics could be made more explicit.", "factor4": "The statement is well-structured with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion, but some sections feel overpacked with examples, limiting deeper reflection.", "factor5": "The language is polished and engaging, with no grammatical errors or awkward phrasing. The tone is academic yet personal, effectively conveying the candidate\u2019s enthusiasm and dedication to history and politics."}

<ps>
Subject: Civil Engineering – Cambridge Offer  From a young age I’ve always been interested in the way things work. Whilst doing a week’s work experience at BDP, an international architectural firm, I learnt more about civil engineering and what it entailed. The mix of design, maths and physics really appeals to me, as does the need for engineers to be creative thinkers who can solve problems. I believe it’s a career that suits my strengths very well. Throughout year 12 I have taken part in the engineering enrichment at sixth form. This has involved visits from companies such as Rolls Royce, and visits to JLR. One of the major projects we have done was with PM Group; they gave us a brief to design a kiosk and then in teams we worked over 5 weeks to fulfil the brief including researching materials, cost and looking into the environmental impact. We then presented our findings to their lead engineers. Led by me as team leader, our team won due to our succinct presentation that focused on the design and target market. I have also enjoyed the mechanics modules I studied in maths and physics, and I am looking forward to doing M2 in further maths this year. At school I enjoyed taking part in the UKMT challenges every year; they provided maths questions outside those in the curriculum that really made me think. I have achieved 6 Gold and 5 Best in school certificates and progressed to the next round each year. In year 11 I was selected to go on a summer school at the University of Oxford as one of the top 200 scorers in the UK. This led to me receiving the award for Outstanding Maths Student in the Borough 2014 awards. The summer school was a fantastic experience with lecturers from different universities. It was an engaging week that really challenged me by introducing topics such as combinatorics and number theory, and helped me to decide that I prefer the applied maths used in engineering compared to pure maths. I particularly enjoyed the tutorial session I had with Victor Flynn, it helped me to see how I could change my approach to questions and experience the supervision system first hand. Last year I built on this experience by attending a 10 week course ran by the FMSP at Warwick Uni; to challenge myself further I have started looking at STEP papers and aim to progress to the BMO this year. Out of college I am part of the Scout Association. I volunteer twice a week and really enjoy helping the children learn new things in a fun environment, and it has improved my leadership skills and ability to work as part of a team. Last summer I was lucky enough to attend the 23rd World Scout Jamboree in Japan. It was an amazing experience with 30,000 Scouts from 159 countries camping for 2 weeks together sharing our different cultures. We spent some time in Tokyo and while there I was amazed by the infrastructure; from the Sky Tree to Rainbow Bridge. A key part to all buildings in Japan is that they need to be earthquake proof. The Sky tree’s design uses ideas from pagodas; traditional Japanese houses, and the roots of trees to make it safe during earthquakes. How to make a design earthquake proof is something that really interests me, and in particular, the role the natural frequency of the building has to play so as to avoid resonance with the frequency of an earthquake. To pursue this interest further I read Structures: Or why things don’t fall down. I have completed my Bronze and Silver DofE and I am in the process of finishing off the last sections on my Gold DofE, alongside this I am working towards my Queen’s Scout Award, the highest award in Scouting. As part of this I played my cornet in a jazz band and played cricket for the school team, the highlight being when our team made it to the National Indoor finals at Lords and came 3rd overall. I have also won various trophies as part of a team in walking competitions such as the Sun Run (26miles) and Six Dales (35 miles) and enjoy taking part in activities such as kayaking and climbing with Scouts.   Total: 33 / 35                              
</ps>

**Scores**:
<scores>
{"factor1": 9, "factor2": 10, "factor3": 5, "factor4": 4, "factor5": 5}
</scores>

{"factor1": "The statement demonstrates strong motivation for studying engineering, with specific examples of experiences that solidified the candidate's interest. For instance, the week of work experience at BDP and the engineering enrichment projects show a clear connection between personal interests, skills, and the field of study. Motivation is compelling and well-tailored to the subject, but it could delve slightly deeper into long-term career aspirations to reach full marks.", "factor2": "The statement provides detailed and relevant experiences, including engineering enrichment programs, leadership in team projects (e.g., PM Group kiosk design), and participation in advanced maths challenges like UKMT and FMSP at Warwick. Achievements such as winning borough awards and progressing to high-level competitions further illustrate the candidate\u2019s suitability for engineering. This is a comprehensive showcase of preparation and enthusiasm.", "factor3": "The candidate highlights strong leadership skills, teamwork, and problem-solving abilities through their role in Scouts and as a team leader in the engineering project. They also demonstrate resilience and determination through their DofE achievements and personal challenges like STEP preparation. The examples are specific and directly relevant to engineering.", "factor4": "The statement is well-organized, with distinct thematic sections covering motivation, experience, skills, and extracurricular activities. However, some transitions (e.g., between Scouts and infrastructure in Tokyo) could be smoother to ensure the narrative flows more cohesively. Overall, it is easy to follow and logically structured.", "factor5": "The language is polished, professional, and engaging, with varied sentence structures and a confident tone. Technical terms such as \"natural frequency\" and references to books like Structures: Or Why Things Don\u2019t Fall Down enhance the academic tone. There are no grammatical errors or awkward phrasing."}

<ps>
PS Graded Essay  Subject: Accounting – Average/high   As Confucius said: “Choose a job you love, and you will never have to work a day in your life.” This quote made me realise the importance of choosing the right profession and what impact this choice would have on my life. I developed an interest in the field of accounting through my exposure to it at school. We have analysed and interpreted financial statements of various companies like: Cashbuild and Enron. This has given me the opportunity to explore the basics of bookkeeping and to see how organisations function, within the corporate world. What is fascinating about accounting as a profession is that it is multi-faceted and one can easily move into fields linked to it namely: banking, taxation, insurance and auditing. It is also very interesting how one cannot only use accounting rules to control and drive systems within an organisation but also to steer companies out of an economic crisis. Therefore, I look forward to being part of this dynamic field of study and ensuring that the company I work for in the future is on the correct path towards financial stability and security.  I have participated in various accounting workshops and I have work experience in accounting firms like PwC and Grant Thornton. These two accounting firms are very different as PwC works with big companies, while Grant Thornton works with smaller organisations. Working in these two environments gave me the opportunity to realise what auditing is all about and what the profession entails as well as what is expected of me as a member of the profession. My exposure to that has also given me the opportunity to strengthen my public speaking and communication skills.  I moved to South Africa, from Greece, 5 years ago, which was a big change for me, not only culturally but also on a personal level. However, I adapted quickly to the new environment and got used to the new way of life. South Africa is a country with a variety of races and through my attendance at a multi-cultural school, I have learned to be open-minded and interact successfully with people of all races and cultures. I am an optimistic, passionate and hard working person, and this has always motivated me to always finish what I start, regardless of what happens. One of my strengths is my determination to succeed and I am very task-orientated. I pride myself on my organisational skills and am confident that my choice of study, on a tertiary level, is well-suited to my strengths.  I was awarded Honours in my school and I helped in my school’s library as well as the organisation of the school’s Dance Show. In addition, I have obtained the B1 Delf Exam in French. Outside of academic pursuits, I have played the piano since the age of 7. I have passed various exams including Grade 7 Royal School Piano Exam and the Grade 6 Royal School Theory Exam. I have participated in Concerto Festivals where I performed with the accompaniment of the University of Pretoria Orchestra. In addition, I have joined an organisation called “Hand in Hand” which organises outings to orphanages, shelter homes and hospitals. I thoroughly enjoy assisting members of the community that are less fortunate than myself and have a passion for children, helping them in areas where there is a need. I am computer literate (Microsoft – all programmes) and I also enjoy horse riding and playing tennis with friends as these activities help me relax, especially during the exam period.  I have chosen to study in the UK for the following three reasons. Firstly, in my opinion, the UK has the best universities in the world and being accepted in one of these universities will help me realise my dream of becoming an Accountant. Secondly, studying in UK will open many doors in my career and I will be able to work there and therefore gain valuable experience. The third reason is that I love meeting new people and discovering new things, hence I believe that studying in UK will make me a more open-minded and motivated individual.  Total: 28/ 35                                
</ps>

**Scores**:
<scores>
{"factor1": 7, "factor2": 9, "factor3": 4, "factor4": 4, "factor5": 4}
</scores>

{"factor1": "The statement demonstrates clear motivation and relevance to accounting, particularly through references to analyzing financial statements and workshops at PwC and Grant Thornton. However, while the motivation is evident, it could benefit from deeper insights into why the candidate is passionate about accounting beyond career prospects.", "factor2": "The personal statement highlights highly relevant and specific experiences, including work at PwC and Grant Thornton, which align directly with the accounting field. The contrast between working in large and small firms is insightful. Additional examples like school honors and musical achievements further demonstrate a well-rounded profile.", "factor3": "Relevant skills such as public speaking, communication, and adaptability (from moving countries) are mentioned. However, the examples, while present, could be elaborated further to explicitly link them to the requirements of accounting, such as teamwork or analytical thinking in specific scenarios.", "factor4": "The statement is logically structured, with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion. However, some transitions between paragraphs could be smoother, particularly when moving from professional experiences to personal qualities. The thematic sections are clear but lack a cohesive progression throughout.", "factor5": "The language is polished, with some grammatical errors. Vocabulary is varied, and the statement maintains a professional tone throughout. The presentation is clear, and the structure adheres to expectations for a strong personal statement."}
